user_prompt: Implement `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileDecoder.cpp` and its associated header file. It should be a class object
Reference `file_packet_t`, `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileRecorder.cpp`, `scripts\thefly_audio_decoder.py`
The constructor dictates what the input file name is, and registers callbacks
- on complete
- on progress
The class itself has public functions for
- start
- cancel
- poll
- getting the state
- getting the progress
- the destructor must clean up correctly, there will be FIFOs involved, larger than what the stack allows
The start function will spin up a FreeRTOS thread, the thread is private, give it a stack size of 2048
the on-complete callback must be fired from poll, not the new thread
all actual file and file system operations must be contained within the new thread
the decoding strategy is a bit simpler than the python script

instantiate two AudioFIFO instances, each with 2048 capacity. One for left channel and one for right channel. The rule is: `file_packet_t` has a field `src`. If `src` is an odd number, the data goes into the left channel (this should be mic), if `src` is an even number, the data goes into the right channel (this should be BT).
The FIFOâ€™s watermark feature should be used, both set to 512 samples (1024 bytes).
open the input file

open the output file as â€œtemp.wavâ€, write a fake WAV file RIFF header inside, once we are finished, this will be overwritten. make sure the file write seek pointer is now at the start of stereo 16 bit PCM data.
Loop
If both FIFOs can accept at least 512 more samples, then decode a packet, and enqueue it into the correct FIFO. (reminder that the maximum samples per packet is 256, although a size of 240 is likely)

Then, remembering that the watermark feature is used, we have three possible conditions: if only the left FIFO allows for dequeuing, if only the right FIFO allows for dequeuing, or if both FIFOs allow for dequeuing.
If both can be dequeued, then do the dequeue 128 samples from each at a time, interleaved into stereo PCM data and written to the output file.
If only one FIFO can be dequeued, then dequeue 128 samples and create stereo PCM data with the other channel silent, write it to the output file
If the file has been completely read to end, then set the watermark to 0 and continue dequeuing until both FIFOs are empty.
Then the loop is finished. Write the RIFF header correctly. Close the file â€œtemp.wavâ€ and then rename it to the proper name.
Set a flag saying it is complete. Break out of the loop. Delete the thread.

Progress report call back is called every 500ms, but also, only check if that time has elapsed every 8 packet being decoded. Progress is reported by the input file seek pointer vs the input file size.
A cancel flag means the temporary file is deleted and then the loop exits, the thread then ends correctly
Implement decryption as needed according to security level
agent_reply: Also added `AudioFifo::setWatermark()` in [AudioFifo.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h) so the decoder can drop the watermark to zero for EOF draining.
Both builds pass. I left unrelated worktree changes alone, including the existing test/developer-docs files.

user_prompt: I want to make a change to logic of the decoding

Previously I said: "If both can be dequeued, then do the dequeue 128 samples from each at a time, interleaved into stereo PCM data and written to the output file. If only one FIFO can be dequeued, then dequeue 128 samples and create stereo PCM data with the other channel silent, write it to the output file"

I want to amend that, if both FIFOs had data, and we dequeue from both, and then one becomes empty, then continue dequeuing the one that still has data and write it into the file as well (but as stereo, with the other channel silent), do this until both are empty.
agent_reply: Implemented the decode drain change in [AudioFileDecoder.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileDecoder.cpp:808).

Now, when both FIFOs are ready at the start of a dequeue pass, the decoder keeps draining until both are empty. If one side runs out first, it continues writing the remaining side as stereo PCM with silence on the empty channel.

agent_reply: That crash was very likely the decoder task stack. The task had 2048 bytes as requested, but `taskMain()` was putting path buffers, `FsFile` objects, and security context on that stack. The dumpâ€™s crashed task SP was right near the stack floor, with a corrupted backtrace.

I fixed it in [AudioFileDecoder.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileDecoder.cpp:44): the decoder task now uses a heap-backed `DecoderTaskContext` for paths, file handles, and GCM state. Cleanup still happens inside the decoder thread, and the FreeRTOS task stack remains `2048`.
Also kept the amended FIFO drain behavior intact.

user_prompt: increase the stack size, 4096
make sure the FIFOs don't sit on stack
agent_reply: Done. The decoder task stack is now `4096` in [AudioFileDecoder.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileDecoder.cpp:29).

I also checked the FIFO ownership: both decoder FIFOs are heap allocated with `new (std::nothrow) AudioFifo(...)`, and their internal sample buffers are allocated by `AudioFifo::begin()`, so they are not sitting on the task stack.
Done. The decoder task stack is now `4096` in [AudioFileDecoder.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileDecoder.cpp:29).

I also checked the FIFO ownership: both decoder FIFOs are heap allocated with `new (std::nothrow) AudioFifo(...)`, and their internal sample buffers are allocated by `AudioFifo::begin()`, so they are not sitting on the task stack.

user_prompt: find me places in the code where the RIFF header is involved, look for mentions of 44, which is the length of the header, unless I am wrong about how we've implemented
agent_reply: Youâ€™re right: in our C++ implementation, the RIFF/WAV header is treated as a fixed **44-byte canonical PCM header**.
Recorder writes a fixed RIFF/WAVE header, including `RIFF`, `WAVE`, `fmt `, and `data`.
Recorder seeks past the 44-byte header before writing audio data.
Decoder writes a fixed 44-byte WAV header for decoded output.
Checks for `RIFF` / `WAVE`, but then walks chunks instead of assuming the audio starts at byte 44.
Validates the RIFF/WAVE wrapper when appending metadata, but doesnâ€™t hardcode `44` there.

One caveat: `PlaybackView` / `WavPlayback` assumes audio data begins at byte 44 and that everything after that is audio. That is true for the WAVs we write with the fixed header, but it would be wrong for WAV files with extra chunks before `data`, and it may slightly overcount if metadata chunks are appended after audio data.

user_prompt: ### Task: major change to recorded encrypted file format
The current implementation uses nearly standard `*.wav` files when unencrypted (lowest security level builds), this is easy to stream and play back.

The current implementation uses a custom format that uses fixed sized packets but each packet is variable length internally, and itâ€™s all encrypted. While this packet can also carry metadata and can be extended to carry event data, it is highly unlikely that the advantages of the flexibility is worth it over the disadvantage of it being difficult to play back in the same way, with easily calculatable audio durations and the ability to seek within a file. The file size is smaller but microSD card performance benchmarks shows that we do not need the smaller file size.
So we are going to throw out the whole custom format idea. We are just going to make encrypted `*.wav` files but call them `*.rec` files.
The document `developer-docs\recording-file-format.md` has been updated and is considered current.
A lot of stuff needs to be removed from `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileRecorder.cpp` , but the new `*.wav` writing functions only need very small changes for this to work.
There needs to be two static buffers, one for the size of a chunk of plaintext audio, and the second buffer for the encrypted audio, before it gets written to the file.

Remove `AudioFileDecoder.cpp` and the associated header file completely. Donâ€™t bother editing them to make them work. Benchmarks have shown that they are incredibly slow and the user will never use them. But we will offer live playback, which is fast because the bottleneck is actually just microSD card writing.
The sizes of the plaintext chunk and the result encrypted chunks shall be defined in `inc\defs.h` so the code can use them later, include also the encrypted RIFF header size.
Do not yet edit the playback related code, do not edit the python scripts. Weâ€™ll do that later
agent_reply: Implemented the encrypted recording format change.
- Added shared encrypted WAV chunk sizing in [inc/defs.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:12):
`WAV_ENCRYPTED_AUDIO_PLAINTEXT_LENGTH = 1024`, encrypted audio chunk length, and encrypted RIFF header length.

- Reworked [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:90) so security level 0 still writes normal `.wav`, while security level 1+ writes `.rec` as encrypted WAV-shaped chunks.
- Encrypted `.rec` files now write the RIFF header as the first encrypted chunk, then fixed-size encrypted PCM chunks.
- Final partial encrypted audio chunks are padded with silence so chunk math stays fixed.
- Removed the old packet format definitions from `defs.h`.
I did not edit playback or Python scripts for this pass. `lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.h` is still dirty from the earlier shared `WAV_RIFF_HEADER_LENGTH` change, not from this task.

- Search confirms old packet/decoder identifiers are gone from code.
One note: `git diff --check` only complains about trailing whitespace in the already-updated `developer-docs/recording-file-format.md`; I left that doc untouched.
